voice_guide_docs.label: What Is Fluid
voice_guide_docs.content:
# What Is Fluid / We-Commerce

## The Core Problem

Most e-commerce platforms were built for anonymous commerce. A stranger finds a product through an ad, clicks a link, checks out as a guest, and leaves. The platform never needed to know who referred them, who should be compensated, or whether that customer was connected to a network of relationships that extended well beyond a single transaction.

Direct selling is the opposite of that. Every transaction in direct selling exists within a web of human relationships — distributors, customers, sponsors, downlines. Who gets credit matters. How the order flows through a compensation plan matters. Whether a rep's effort three weeks ago is recognized when a customer finally buys matters enormously.

Shopify doesn't know any of this. Neither does Stripe. Or Square. They were built for a different problem.

When direct selling companies try to run their businesses on platforms designed for anonymous commerce, the gaps are not minor inconveniences. Roughly half of the transactions a direct selling company needs to process — enrollments with compensation logic, logged-in subscription orders, downline purchases — cannot be natively handled by Shopify at all. The rest are handled imperfectly: broken attribution, missed subscriptions, failed international transactions, disconnected commission engines.

The result is a company spending its energy keeping fragile systems alive instead of growing its business.

---

## What Fluid Is

Fluid is the We-Commerce platform — the only complete commerce platform purpose-built for direct selling.

We-Commerce is the category Fluid has defined: commerce that is built on relationships, not transactions. Where brands and sellers win together. Where every piece of content can be shared by a real person with real attribution. Where a rep's effort is recognized, not lost.

Fluid is not Shopify plus a bunch of plugins. It is a unified system — commerce, payments, attribution, sync, web experience, rep tools, and extensibility — built from the ground up for how direct selling actually works.

---

## The Platform: Seven Integrated Features

### Fluid Connect
The data synchronization layer. Keeps a company's back-office commission engine (Exigo, ByDesign, Pillars, etc.) continuously aligned with the Fluid commerce platform. Bi-directional, automatic, real-time. One connection replaces a tangle of custom scripts and fragile exports.

### Fluid Payments
The payment infrastructure. Built for the higher-risk classification of direct selling, with multi-processor architecture, cascading payment retries, decline recovery, network tokens, and a full suite of alternative payment methods for global markets. Fluid Pay is the platform's native saved payment method. Every transaction is treated as if it matters — because in direct selling, it does.

### FairShare
The attribution system. Tracks the full customer journey — shared links, live streams, media engagement, follow-ups — and ensures credit goes to the reps who created the outcome. Five configurable attribution settings. An audit trail called the Order Journey. Applied automatically to every page of the website. The foundation of trust between reps and corporate.

### Checkout
The full commerce transaction layer. Products, subscriptions, enrollments, promo codes, order management — all built for the complexity of direct selling. Country-level variant pricing (not natively available in Shopify). Flexible subscription billing with independent billing, fulfillment, and volume frequencies. Enrollment flows with international pre-seeding. Order on Behalf Of for assisted-selling markets.

### Builder (Web Builder)
The visual website creation tool. Corporate teams build and manage the public website — pages, layouts, product displays, campaign experiences — without engineering dependency. Every page built in Builder automatically carries FairShare attribution, meaning reps can share any page on the site and credit will follow. Multiple themes can coexist, with one live at a time.

### App (Rep Tools)
The rep-facing business tool, available on mobile and desktop. Reps share content, manage contacts, follow up through Smart Links, and access Catchups — an AI-powered notification system that surfaces business opportunities proactively. Available in a standard version and a Co-founder (pro) tier with Unified Messaging, a personal business phone number, and advanced AI features. Corporate customizes the app experience through the App Builder and Mobile App Builder.

### Droplets
The extensibility layer. An open API system and marketplace — currently 20–30 Droplets available — that allows companies, partners, and developers to build new capabilities inside the Fluid platform. Tax tools (Avalara, Sovos), fulfillment (ShipStation, ShipHero), back office connections, and more. Droplets inherit the Fluid foundation automatically: attribution, payments, and sync remain intact. The Shopify App Store equivalent for direct selling, with theoretically unlimited extensibility.

---

## The Fluid Philosophy: Every Transaction Matters

In direct selling, a single transaction is never just a transaction.

A new customer might become a distributor. That distributor might become a leader. That leader might build a team of hundreds. It starts with one order — the first one that works.

When a transaction fails in this world, the cost is not a lost sale. It is a relationship that didn't start. A career that didn't launch. A rep who learned that the system doesn't work for them, and who will share less because of it.

Fluid was built around the belief that every transaction deserves to be treated as if it matters. Not most transactions. Not the easy ones. Every single one.

---

## The We-Commerce Flywheel

Direct selling companies have a built-in advantage that D2C brands spend millions trying to manufacture: a distribution channel of real people who genuinely believe in what they're selling.

But that advantage only works when reps trust the system enough to use it.

FairShare gives reps the confidence that their attribution will be recognized. Fluid Payments gives companies the confidence that transactions will go through. Builder gives corporate the ability to create content that reps can share immediately. Catchups gives reps the intelligence to follow up at exactly the right moment.

When those pieces work together, the flywheel spins:

Content gets shared → traffic grows → conversions happen → new customers create new content → more content gets shared.

Powered not by an ad budget. By people.

---

## Blitz Week

Fluid launches new clients through an intensive four-day on-site engagement called Blitz Week. Client teams come to Fluid headquarters in Lehi, Utah and work side-by-side with Fluid's executives, developers, and creative team to deploy their full platform by the end of the week.

This is not a demo or a planning session. Companies leave Blitz Week with a live, production-ready platform — not a prototype.

Blitz Week exists because one of the biggest barriers to switching platforms is migration fear. Blitz Week removes that fear by proving it doesn't need to take months. It takes four days.

For partners on a term agreement, Blitz Week is included at no additional cost.

---

## Who Fluid Is Built For

Fluid is built for direct selling and affiliate commerce companies — companies where the relationship between the brand, the distributor network, and the customer is the product. Companies where every transaction carries compound potential. Companies for whom Shopify was never the right answer, even when it was the most familiar one.

voice_guide_docs.label: The Problem We're Solving
voice_guide_docs.content:
# The Problem We're Solving

## The Traffic Problem in Direct Selling

Traffic is the lifeblood of growth online. D2C brands never stop creating content — fresh UGC, new ads, quick stories, testimonials, FAQs — because once someone has seen a piece of content, it's stale to them. Freshness is the oxygen that keeps the flywheel alive.

Direct selling companies are stuck. Their reps aren't sharing — not because the products aren't good, but because the system has failed them enough times that they've stopped trusting it.

They sent someone to the corporate website. Someone else got credit. They built a workaround. The workaround broke. They stopped using the platform. Now they share their own screenshots and homemade links, or they stop sharing altogether.

And without reps in motion, traffic dies.

## The Trust Problem

The deeper issue is not technology. It's trust.

Reps have been burned. Attribution didn't work. Their effort wasn't recognized. They learned, rationally, that the risk of sharing corporate content wasn't worth taking. So they stopped.

When reps stop sharing, the company's marketing investment — every video, every product page, every campaign — goes unactivated. It sits in a Google Drive folder no one opens.

This is the problem Fluid was built to solve: not just making the tools better, but making the system trustworthy enough that reps believe using it is worth their time.

## The Shopify Gap

Roughly half of the transactions a direct selling company needs to process cannot be natively handled by Shopify.

Not because Shopify is bad software. Because it was built for a different problem. Shopify was designed for anonymous guest checkout — a stranger finds a product, clicks buy, and leaves. The platform has no concept of rep attribution, compensation logic, enrollment flows, or logged-in subscription commerce tied to a downline structure.

Direct selling companies attempting to run on Shopify are forced to stitch together plugins, custom code, and manual processes to approximate what they need. The result is fragility: broken attribution when an integration fails, missed commissions when a sync drops, subscription lapses when a payment error isn't recovered, and marketing content that reps don't trust to share because the link might not work.

The platform everyone says direct selling companies should use literally cannot handle the majority of what direct selling companies need to do.

## The Integration Tax

Most direct selling companies are not running on one platform. They are running on 10 to 15: a payment processor, a commission engine, a site builder, a mobile app tool, a CRM, a fulfillment provider, an email platform, a subscription management tool. Each with its own contract. Each with its own integration point. Each representing a new place for something to break.

The teams responsible for managing these integrations spend enormous time and energy keeping them alive — not building, not growing, not serving their distributors. Just maintaining the infrastructure.

Every integration failure has a cost beyond the direct operational impact. When a sync drops and commissions are wrong, reps lose faith in the system. When a transaction fails and no one knows why, confidence erodes. When a promo code doesn't work correctly because two systems have conflicting data, customers and reps both have a worse experience.

The integration tax is paid in time, in trust, and in distributor retention.

## The Rep Retention Problem

The best distributors in any direct selling company are the ones with options. And when the tools don't work — when attribution fails, when checkout is clunky, when the app feels like it was built for a different era — those distributors leave.

They don't always leave loudly. Often they just slow down. They stop recruiting. They stop sharing. They process their own orders and keep their checks, but they stop building. And slowly, the growth engine stalls.

Technology is not the only driver of rep retention. But technology that fails reps in visible ways — that loses their attribution, that makes checkout feel unreliable, that gives them no visibility into who is engaging with their content — actively accelerates attrition.

## What The Solution Looks Like

The solution is not a better plugin. It is a platform built from the ground up for how direct selling actually works.

One system where attribution is automatic and trustworthy. Where payments are optimized for the higher-risk classification of the industry. Where enrollment flows handle international complexity natively. Where subscription billing is flexible enough for any compensation structure. Where reps have real tools that respect how they work. Where corporate can build and launch without engineering bottlenecks. Where everything is connected — not stitched together.

That is what Fluid is.

Not a better version of what existed before. A different category entirely: We-Commerce — commerce built on relationships, where the technology finally reflects how direct selling actually works.

voice_guide_docs.label: Why WeCommerce Exists
voice_guide_docs.content:
# Why We-Commerce Exists

## Commerce changed. The platforms didn't.

For the last two decades, e-commerce was built around a single assumption: customers arrive alone, click alone, and buy alone. Someone sees an ad. They land on a page. They checkout or they don't. The platform's job is to handle the transaction. Everything else is someone else's problem.

That model works fine if you're selling candles to strangers on Instagram.

Direct selling is something different entirely.

In direct selling, a rep spends weeks building a relationship. She answers questions at 9pm. She shares her own story. She follows up, not because a system told her to, but because she genuinely believes in what she sells. When her contact finally clicks a link and buys — that transaction isn't the beginning of a customer relationship. It's the result of one that already existed.

Traditional e-commerce platforms weren't built for that. They were built for volume, for anonymous traffic, for the kind of commerce where individual transactions are statistics and a 2% failure rate is considered acceptable.

In direct selling, there is no acceptable failure rate. Every transaction represents someone's effort, someone's trust, and often someone's livelihood.

---

## The problem isn't one thing. It's everything not connecting.

Direct selling companies figured this out the hard way. They tried to build on Shopify. They bolted on payment processors, commission engines, attribution tools, mobile apps, and CRM platforms. They hired IT teams to keep the integrations alive. They watched things break in ways no one could explain, because the person who built the sync script left two years ago.

The result: a Frankenstein stack that consumes time, erodes trust, and fights the business instead of serving it.

The pain isn't "I need better payments." It's "I spend all my time keeping this thing alive instead of growing."

And while all of that is happening internally, the reps — the people the entire model depends on — are quietly losing faith. They share a link and someone else gets credit. They send a customer to the website and the checkout breaks. They stop sharing. Traffic dies. Growth stalls.

The technology was supposed to help. Instead it became the obstacle.

---

## We-Commerce is a different idea about what commerce is.

Shopify optimized for traffic. We-Commerce optimized for relationships.

That's not a tagline. It's a structural difference in how the platform is built and what it believes commerce actually is.

We-Commerce starts from the premise that in direct selling, the relationship IS the product. The rep is not a distribution channel — she is the reason the customer trusts the brand. The transaction at the end of the funnel is not the point. It's the proof that the relationship worked.

When you build a platform around that premise, everything changes.

Attribution stops being a technical problem and becomes a trust problem — one worth solving completely, not approximately. Checkout stops being a generic form and becomes the moment where weeks of relationship-building either pay off or evaporate. Content stops being marketing collateral and becomes the currency reps use to share their story and build their audience. Payments stop being a commodity and become the final guardian of everything that came before them.

Every feature in the platform connects back to one thing: making sure the people who do the work get the credit, the tools, and the outcomes they deserve.

---

## The advantage was always there. It just needed the right platform.

Here's what the traditional e-commerce world is now spending billions trying to manufacture: a distribution channel of real people who genuinely believe in what they sell.

Direct selling companies already have that.

They have reps who use the products, know the customers, and share from personal experience. They have relationships that exist before, during, and after the sale. They have trust that can't be faked by an AI chatbot or manufactured by a paid influencer.

In a world where consumer trust in digital content is collapsing — where people can't tell what's real, what's sponsored, and what was generated by software — that built-in human authenticity is one of the most valuable assets in commerce.

We-Commerce exists to unlock it.

Not by adding more tools to a broken stack. But by building the only platform that was designed from the ground up for the way direct selling actually works — where the rep is the engine, the relationship is the foundation, and every transaction is the result of real human effort that deserves to be recognized.

---

## One platform. Built for all of it.

We-Commerce is not a collection of features. It's a complete operating system for direct selling — one where every part was built to work with every other part from day one.

FairShare attribution so reps trust the system and share. Fluid Payments so transactions don't die at the finish line. Fluid Connect so data flows cleanly between the platform and the back office. Builder so the website reflects the brand without waiting on a developer. The Mobile App so reps can run their business from anywhere. Droplets so the platform extends as the business grows.

None of it works in isolation. All of it works together.

That's the point. And that's why We-Commerce exists.

voice_guide_docs.label: Voice and Style Guide
voice_guide_docs.content:
# Fluid / We-Commerce Voice & Style Guide

## Overview

This guide defines how Fluid communicates — the tone, the structure, the words we use, and the patterns we avoid. It exists so that any piece of content created for Fluid, whether a homepage headline, a feature explainer, a social post, or a sales deck, feels like it came from the same place.

The shorthand reference for this voice is **"Brian Chesky style"** — named for how the Airbnb CEO communicates: direct, grounded in specific moments, confident without being arrogant, human without being informal. He doesn't explain what a feature does — he explains what it means. He doesn't list benefits — he tells you what was broken and how it got fixed.

Use this guide alongside the Fluid knowledge base files. Those files tell you *what* is true about Fluid. This file tells you *how to say it*.

---

## The Core Voice

### Short sentences. Declarative statements. No hedging.

The Fluid voice is confident. It does not qualify. It does not say "we believe we can help you" — it says "we built this to fix that." It does not say "solutions" or "offerings" — it says what the thing actually does.

When in doubt, make the sentence shorter.

**Wrong:**
> Fluid offers a comprehensive suite of tools designed to help direct selling companies manage the complex attribution requirements unique to their industry.

**Right:**
> Direct selling has an attribution problem. Reps don't trust the system. So they stop sharing. Fluid fixes that.

---

### Ground everything in a specific moment or real person.

Abstract claims die. Specific moments live. The most effective Fluid writing doesn't describe features — it describes a person in a situation, and then shows what changes.

Consider the difference between these two ways of describing FairShare:

**Abstract:**
> FairShare provides multi-touch attribution that ensures rep credit is maintained across the full customer journey.

**Specific:**
> She shared the link three weeks ago. Answered a question the same night. The customer went quiet — then bought last Tuesday through a Google search. She still got the credit. Because FairShare tracked the whole thing.

The first tells you what the feature does. The second tells you what it means to the person using it. Same capability — completely different impact.

Every feature has a version of this. Find the person. Find the moment. Find what changes when the system actually works.

---

### Name the problem before you name the solution.

Fluid content almost always starts with what's broken. Not to be negative — but because naming the real problem accurately is what makes a reader feel understood. If they feel understood, they'll listen to the solution.

The pattern is: **Here's what's actually happening → Here's why → Here's what we built.**

**Example from the Manifesto:**
> Attribution isn't a technical problem. It's a trust problem.
> Here's the real issue: reps don't trust that they'll get credit.
> So they don't share corporate content. They build their own bootleg versions. Or they just stop sharing altogether.
> Can you blame them?
> We built FairShare to fix this.

Notice: the problem is named, reframed (it's a *trust* problem, not a technical one), made human (can you blame them?), and *then* the solution is introduced. The solution lands harder because the problem was made real first.

**One important balance note:** The problem names the pain — but the writing should not live there. The goal is always to move through the problem toward the possibility on the other side. Direct selling companies have a genuine built-in advantage: a distribution channel of real people who believe in what they sell. That is something D2C brands spend millions trying to manufacture. Fluid exists to unlock that advantage — not just to describe what's broken. When the content earns it, let the upside breathe.

> Content fuels sharing. Sharing fuels traffic. Traffic fuels conversions. Conversions create new content. The flywheel spins — powered not by ads, but by people.

That is the other register of this voice. Problem-first does not mean problem-only.

---

### Say "you" and "we." Not "companies" and "platforms."

Fluid talks directly to the reader. The company being addressed is "you." Fluid is "we." This is not a whitepaper. It is a conversation.

**Wrong:**
> Direct selling companies often struggle with attribution systems that fail to properly credit distributors.

**Right:**
> Your reps aren't sharing because they've been burned. They sent someone to the website. Someone else got credit. They learned the system doesn't work for them. So they stopped.

---

### Write in present tense. Active voice. Always.

**Wrong:**
> Failed transactions have historically been a challenge for direct selling merchants.

**Right:**
> When a transaction fails in your world, it's not a stranger who shrugs and buys somewhere else. It's your rep's sister.

---

## What This Voice Is NOT

### Not hype. Not chest-thumping.

The Fluid voice does not say "industry-leading," "best-in-class," "revolutionary," or "game-changing." Those words are meaningless because every company uses them. If something is genuinely better, show why — don't assert it.

**Wrong:**
> Fluid's industry-leading attribution engine delivers best-in-class results for direct selling companies.

**Right:**
> If your rep planted the seed three weeks ago and the customer finally buys through a Google search, your rep still gets credit.

---

### Not a feature list. Not a bullet dump.

Features are not the story. What the feature means to a real person is the story. You can list capabilities in a knowledge base file — but when writing for marketing, always translate the capability into a human consequence.

**Wrong:**
> FairShare supports first touch, last touch, most active, sponsored ordering, orphan order rules, and enrollment credit attribution.

**Right:**
> FairShare tracks the whole journey. Not just who was last. Not just who owns the site. Whoever actually showed up.

---

### Not corporate. Not cautious.

The Fluid voice is willing to name what's broken — including by name. Shopify is named. The failure modes are described specifically. The voice does not hide behind vague language to avoid controversy.

> About half your transactions can't even happen on Shopify.
> That's not a typo.

That is a bold, specific, verifiable claim — stated directly. That is the Fluid voice.

---

### Not informal either.

The voice is human, not casual. It does not use slang, emoji-speak, or try-hard relatability. It is the voice of someone who has thought deeply about a problem and is sharing what they found — directly, without performance.

---

## Structural Patterns

### Short paragraphs. Often one or two sentences.

White space is not wasted space. Short paragraphs create rhythm. They make the reader keep moving. Long blocks of text signal "this is going to be work." Short blocks signal "stay with me."

### Use sentence fragments deliberately — not constantly.

Fragments create emphasis. They earn their place by slowing the reader down at exactly the right moment. Used too often, they become a tic. The writing starts to feel choppy rather than punchy.

The test: does the fragment land harder than a full sentence would? If yes, use it. If it just reads like an incomplete thought, finish it.

**Works:**
> When reps trust the system, they share. When they share, you get traffic.
> Simple as that.

> Shopify optimized for traffic.
> We-Commerce optimized for relationships.

"Simple as that" and "We-Commerce optimized for relationships" land because full sentences set them up. The fragment is the punctuation — not the whole paragraph.

### Repeat a word or phrase for effect.

The Manifesto does this well:

> We believe every transaction matters.
> We believe the rep who spent three months nurturing a relationship matters.
> We believe the syncs and the attribution and the checkout flow and the landing pages all matter — because they're all places where transactions go to die.

Repetition builds momentum. Use it when you want something to land.

### End sections with a landing statement.

The best Fluid paragraphs don't trail off — they end on something that sticks.

> When reps trust the system, they share. When they share, you get traffic.
> Simple as that.

> Powered not by ads. By people.

> One platform. Every transaction.
> Built for companies who refuse to treat their reps and customers as statistics.

---

## Words to Use

These words and phrases appear throughout strong Fluid content and reflect what the brand actually values:

- **people** — always preferred over "users," "distributors," "merchants"
- **trust** — the central emotional problem Fluid solves
- **credit** — what reps deserve and what the system should give them
- **effort** — what reps put in and what the system should recognize
- **share / sharing** — the core rep behavior the whole platform enables
- **journey** — how customers move toward a purchase (never "funnel" in brand voice)
- **relationship** — what commerce in direct selling is actually built on
- **the system** — used to describe the broken status quo that Fluid replaces
- **fix** — direct selling technology is broken; Fluid fixes it
- **confidence** — what reps need to share, what corporate needs to build

---

## Words to Avoid

These words are banned or heavily discouraged in Fluid marketing content:

| Avoid | Because |
|---|---|
| seamless | Everyone says this. It means nothing. |
| solutions | Vague corporate filler. Say what the thing actually does. |
| leverage | Corporate jargon. Use "use" or just say what you're doing. |
| robust | Says nothing specific. |
| empower | Overused to the point of meaninglessness. |
| utilize | "Use" works fine. |
| synergy | Never. |
| industry-leading | Unverifiable claim. Show, don't assert. |
| best-in-class | Same. |
| game-changing | Same. |
| revolutionary | Same. |
| innovative | Same. |
| ecosystem (overused) | Use sparingly. Don't use it just to sound technical. |
| users | Use "reps," "distributors," "customers," or "people." |
| merchants | Occasionally acceptable in technical contexts; avoid in brand voice. |
| platform (as a throwaway) | "Platform" means something. Don't use it as filler. |
| journey (as a buzzword) | "Customer journey" is overused in marketing. Use "path" or describe the actual sequence. |

---

## Audience-Specific Tone Adjustments

The core voice stays consistent. These are calibration notes for specific audiences.

### Writing for a CEO or Founder
Lean into vision and competitive stakes. They care about whether the technology is a liability or an advantage. Lead with what's possible, not what's fixed.

> Your technology should be a competitive advantage. Not the reason your best reps leave.

### Writing for VP of Sales / Field Leadership
Lead with rep trust and field outcomes. They've seen attribution break. They know what happens when reps lose faith. Speak to that directly.

> Your best reps aren't disengaged. They've been burned. FairShare fixes the underlying problem — not the symptom.

### Writing for COO / Operations
Lead with the cost of the current situation. Quantify where possible. They respond to specificity.

> How many vendors do you manage? How many integration failures did you troubleshoot last quarter? One platform eliminates that math entirely.

### Writing for a Rep (Direct)
Warmer, more personal. Less business-case framing. More "this is for you."

> You put in the work. You shared the link. You followed up. FairShare makes sure you get the credit — automatically, every time, without you having to fight for it.

### Writing Technical Explainers (Knowledge Base / Reference)
This is where the tone relaxes from brand voice into clear, informative prose. Still no jargon. Still present tense. Still direct. But more complete — less punchy, more precise. The knowledge base files in this project are an example of this register.

### Writing Social Visual Headlines (Text on the Graphic Itself)

This is a distinct writing challenge from captions or long-form content. A headline on a graphic has one job: stop the scroll. The reader hasn't committed to anything yet. They haven't read a caption, clicked a link, or decided to care. The visual text is what earns the next two seconds.

At this scale, most of the long-form principles still apply in spirit — but the execution is different.

**Keep it to five to eight words.** The headline should be readable in a glance. If someone has to work to parse it, they won't.

**Make it feel like it knows something they don't.** The best social headlines create a small gap — a tension between what the reader knows and what the headline implies. That gap is what generates the pause.

**Three formats that work for Fluid:**

*The reframe* — Takes something familiar and names it differently. Earns the pause because it feels slightly unexpected.
> Shopify is great. For 20% of your business.
> Reps share more when they trust they'll get credit.
> Attribution is the whole game. Fluid is how you win it.

*The direct statement of belief* — Confident, short, no qualification. Works best when the claim is specific enough to be interesting but broad enough to land without context.
> Every share should get credit.
> Reps don't leave companies. They leave broken systems.
> Commerce built on relationships. Not ad spend.
> The only e-commerce platform built specifically for direct selling.

*The named contrast* — Sets up a before/after or us/them in a single line. Efficient and concrete.
> Built for the sale. And the relationship behind it.
> One platform. Not fifteen.
> Purpose-built for direct selling. Not retrofitted for it.

**What to avoid on visuals:**

*Feature names without context* — "FairShare" or "Fluid Connect" as a standalone headline means nothing to someone who doesn't already know Fluid. Lead with what it does or what it solves, not what it's called.

*Questions that are too broad* — "Want to grow your business?" stops no one. Questions work when they're specific enough to feel like they're aimed at exactly one kind of person.

*Anything that could be from any company* — "Powerful tools for your team" could be Salesforce, Slack, or a software company you've never heard of. Fluid headlines should be ownable — they should feel like they could only come from a company that understands direct selling specifically.

**The insider test:** Would a direct selling executive who has never heard of Fluid read this headline and think "that's written for me"? If yes, you're in the right territory. If it could apply to any SaaS company or any industry, rewrite it.

---

## Canonical Examples

When in doubt, return to these. They represent the voice at its best.

---

**On trust and attribution (from the Manifesto):**
> Attribution isn't a technical problem. It's a trust problem.
> Here's the real issue: reps don't trust that they'll get credit.
> So they don't share corporate content. They build their own bootleg versions. Or they just stop sharing altogether.
> Can you blame them?

---

**On what's at stake (from the Manifesto):**
> When a transaction fails in your world, it's not a stranger who shrugs and buys somewhere else. It's your rep's sister. Her best friend from college. The person she's been nurturing for three months.
> That failed transaction doesn't just cost you a sale. It costs your rep her confidence. It teaches her that the system doesn't work. That her effort doesn't matter.
> She'll share less next time. Maybe she'll stop sharing altogether.

---

**On what We-Commerce is (from the Why We-Commerce Exists file):**
> Most e-commerce platforms were built for ads and anonymous checkout.
> They assume customers arrive alone, click alone, and buy alone.
> We-Commerce was built for something different.
> Shopify optimized for traffic.
> We-Commerce optimized for relationships.

---

**On the flywheel (from The Traffic Problem):**
> Content fuels sharing. Sharing fuels traffic. Traffic fuels conversions. Conversions create new content. The flywheel spins — powered not by ads, but by people.

---

**On simplicity of purpose (from the Manifesto):**
> When everything syncs correctly, nobody notices. That's the point.

---

## One-Sentence Summary of the Voice

Write like someone who has thought deeply about a broken industry, fixed a real part of it, and wants to tell you exactly what they found — without wasting your time.

voice_guide_docs.label: Builder
voice_guide_docs.content:
# Builder (Web Builder)

## What It Is

Builder — also referred to as Web Builder — is Fluid's visual website creation and management tool. It is where corporate teams control everything customers and reps see on the public-facing website: pages, layouts, product displays, campaign experiences, enrollment flows, and more.

**Naming note:** The correct product names are "Builder" or "Web Builder" depending on context. Do not use "Theme Editor" or "Theme Builder" — these terms underrepresent both the simplicity of the drag-and-drop interface and the depth of control available to more technical users.

Builder is not a standalone site tool. It is the rendering and presentation layer for the entire Fluid platform — the place where products, enrollments, pages, media, and commerce flows are styled and surfaced to the world. What products look like. How enrollment pages are structured. What customers see when they land from a rep's shared link.

---

## What Builder Is (and Isn't)

The **public website** is the central hub for customers and reps — where purchases happen, where the company is experienced, where links from FairShare lead. Builder is the tool corporate teams use to design, build, and update that website.

Builder is not the source of products, pricing, subscriptions, or attribution logic. Those live in other parts of the Fluid platform (Checkout, FairShare, Payments). Builder determines how those elements are displayed and experienced on the public site.

Think of it this way: Fluid manages what's true (products, prices, attribution). Builder manages how it looks and how customers interact with it.

---

## Key Capabilities

### Drag-and-Drop Editing
Corporate teams can build and modify pages without engineering support. Anyone who can use a standard productivity tool can use Builder. Changes are visible in real time and can be reviewed before publishing.

### Section Library
Builder includes a collection of pre-built, high-converting sections — product showcases, testimonials, call-to-action blocks, enrollment flows, FAQs, media displays — that can be dropped into any page and customized. This significantly reduces build time and ensures design quality for teams without dedicated design resources.

### Multiple Themes, One Active at a Time
Companies can maintain multiple themes within Builder simultaneously. Only one theme is "live" (published) at any point, but others can be built, previewed, and held in reserve. Switching which theme is active does not destroy the content or configuration of other themes. This makes seasonal redesigns, campaign launches, and A/B experimentation between full site experiences possible without risk.

### FairShare Attribution on Every Page
This is one of Builder's most important and underappreciated capabilities. FairShare is applied globally through Builder, meaning every page built in the web editor automatically carries FairShare attribution. A rep can share any page on the website — the homepage, a product quiz, the "Shop All" page, a brand storytelling page — and FairShare will track and credit any resulting purchase.

This means attribution is not limited to product pages or custom rep links. It extends to the entire web experience.

### Version History and Rollback
All changes in Builder are versioned. If an edit causes a problem, previous versions are accessible and restorable. There are multiple layers of protection in place to prevent unintended content loss.

### No Developer Required for Updates
Product launches, campaign pages, promotional banners, layout changes — all can be executed by a marketing or operations team member directly in Builder without filing a developer ticket. This eliminates one of the most common bottlenecks in direct selling marketing operations.

### Internationalization
Builder supports multi-language content and international layout configurations. Pages can be localized for different markets without duplicating the entire site structure.

### Performance
Builder-powered pages are built for fast load times globally, including in high-traffic markets where page speed has a direct impact on conversion.

---

## What Builder Connects To

Because Builder is the presentation layer for the full Fluid platform, everything built in it is already connected to:

- **FairShare** — every page, automatically, via global embed
- **Fluid Payments** — checkout flows built in Builder inherit the full payment infrastructure
- **Products and Checkout** — product pages, enrollment flows, and subscription sign-ups are built and styled in Builder using live platform data
- **Live Shopping** — live stream layouts and post-stream content pages are created and managed in Builder

This is different from a generic site builder that requires separate integrations for payments, attribution, or product data. In Builder, those connections exist by default.

---

## Corporate Control with Field Flexibility

Builder supports a guardrails model for the rep experience. Corporate defines what is allowed — the brand standards, the content parameters, the approved sections. Reps personalize within those boundaries. This keeps brand consistency high while giving reps meaningful ownership over how they present the company.

Corporate can build and evolve the experience without creating fragmentation. When the website is updated, every rep's attributed links and replicated site experience reflects the update automatically. No rep is left behind on an outdated version.

---

## Competitive Context

Most direct selling companies either rely on an agency or internal development team to manage website changes, or use a generic site builder that is disconnected from their commerce infrastructure. Both approaches create bottlenecks: either the marketing team cannot move quickly, or the site experience is disconnected from attribution, payments, and product logic.

Builder eliminates both problems. Corporate teams move at the speed of the business. And everything they build is already connected to the rest of the platform — no integration required.

voice_guide_docs.label: Checkout
voice_guide_docs.content:
# Checkout

## What It Is

Fluid's Checkout is the full commerce transaction layer of the We-Commerce platform. It covers everything that happens from the moment a customer decides to buy through the moment an order is fulfilled — including products, pricing, subscriptions, enrollments, promo codes, and order management.

Checkout is purpose-built for direct selling. It handles the complexity that generic e-commerce platforms cannot: country-specific variant pricing, enrollment flows with compensation logic, subscription billing tied to commission engines, and ordering on behalf of others. All of this works out of the box, without plugins or custom development.

---

## Products

### Country-Level Variant Pricing
Fluid supports pricing at the variant level per country. A single product can have different prices for different markets — not just different currencies, but genuinely different prices — applied at the variant level. This is not natively available in Shopify, which may support country pricing through extensions or apps but does not offer it as a core platform feature.

This matters significantly for direct selling companies operating across multiple markets, where pricing strategies differ by region and cannot be treated as a simple currency conversion.

### Variants, Bundles, and Collections
Fluid supports full variant management (size, flavor, format, etc.), product bundles, collections, and category structures. Products can be organized into theme-based templates and display natively across the web experience with SEO optimization and translation support for international markets.

---

## Subscriptions

Subscriptions are a critical component of direct selling commerce. Fluid has invested heavily in subscription infrastructure and is at the forefront of subscription capabilities in the industry.

### Plan Flexibility
Each subscription plan supports independent configuration of three separate frequencies: billing frequency (how often the customer is charged), fulfillment frequency (how often the order ships), and volume frequency (how often CV/QV is applied for commission purposes). These can all be different from each other. This level of flexibility allows client companies to design any pricing strategy and subscription cadence their compensation plan requires.

Pricing on subscription plans can be set as a percentage discount from the standard product price, or as a fully separate subscription-specific price.

### Subscription Bundling
Reps and customers can opt into subscription bundling, which consolidates multiple separate subscriptions into a single billing date. Bundling applies to billing date, payment method, and volume assignment — each can be managed independently. This reduces the number of individual transactions and often saves customers on shipping and tax. Subscriptions remain individually manageable despite being bundled for billing.

### Subscription Recovery
Fluid includes dedicated subscription recovery logic within Fluid Payments. If a recurring subscription charge fails, the system works to recover it — not just retry once and lapse. Subscription recovery is closely integrated with Fluid Payments' decline recovery infrastructure.

### Subscription Management Portal
Customers, reps, and admins all have access to subscription management tools. Customers can pause, skip, cancel, change products, update payment methods, and modify their subscription cadence. All actions available to the customer are also available to administrators in the admin panel.

---

## Enrollments

Enrollment is one of the most important and complex transaction types in direct selling — and one of the areas where generic e-commerce platforms fall shortest. Fluid's enrollment infrastructure is a significant selling point.

### Enrollment Pack Flexibility
Enrollment packs can include one-time products, subscription products, or both. The company configures which products are included, at what pricing, and in what combination.

### Customizable Enrollment Forms
Enrollment forms are built directly in the Fluid admin UI. Forms can be configured to capture information either before or after payment, with fully customizable fields. Both required and optional custom agreements (terms, income disclosures, policies) can be attached to the enrollment flow.

### International Enrollment Support
Enrollment flows are built to handle international markets. Pricing, product availability, forms, and agreements can all be configured by country. Fluid provides pre-seeded enrollment forms and agreements for countries that have been activated in the admin, reducing the setup burden for companies expanding into new markets. This is one of Fluid's strongest selling points for internationally operating direct selling companies.

---

## Promo Codes

Fluid supports a full-featured promotional engine with significant configurability:

- **Discount types:** Percentage off, fixed dollar amount, free products, free shipping
- **Availability controls:** By country, by customer type (retail customer vs. distributor vs. new enrollment), by product, by collection
- **Timing:** Configurable start and end dates
- **Application:** Can be applied manually by the customer or triggered automatically based on cart conditions
- **Stacking:** Multiple promo codes can be applied to a single order

---

## Order on Behalf Of

Reps can place orders on behalf of a customer or contact and assign credit to a specific rep in the process — including themselves, or another rep if appropriate. This applies to both one-time orders and subscription orders. When credit is assigned to a rep on a subscription order, all subsequent recurring orders from that subscription will continue to carry that rep attribution.

Order on Behalf Of is particularly common in non-US markets where assisted ordering by a distributor is standard practice.

---

## Order Management

The admin provides a full order management interface including:

- Order history, statistics, and filtering
- Refunds — calculated automatically, applicable item by item or as a manual amount
- Fulfillment management — including split and held fulfillment for multi-item orders, with configurable shipping notification emails and tracking information
- Inventory management — same status framework as Shopify, with bulk edit options and warehouse support
- Order Journey — the FairShare attribution audit trail attached to every order (see FairShare documentation)

**Returns are not yet supported.** This capability is in development.

---

## Competitive Context

The combination of country-level variant pricing, enrollment-native flows, flexible subscription billing, and Order on Behalf Of makes Fluid's Checkout functionally incompatible with what generic e-commerce platforms offer. These are not features that can be replicated with plugins. They require a commerce layer that was designed for direct selling from the ground up.

Shopify's checkout was designed for anonymous guest transactions. Fluid's checkout was designed for logged-in commerce — enrolled distributors, repeat subscribers, and customers with relationships to specific reps. Those are fundamentally different problems.

voice_guide_docs.label: Droplets
voice_guide_docs.content:
# Droplets

## What It Is

Droplets are Fluid's extensibility layer — the open API and marketplace system that allows companies, partners, and developers to build new capabilities, integrations, and experiences directly inside the We-Commerce platform.

The simplest comparison: Droplets are to Fluid what the App Store is to Shopify. A growing marketplace of installable capabilities, built by Fluid and by third parties, that extend the platform without fragmenting it.

But Droplets are more than an integration catalog. They are the mechanism by which Fluid becomes infinitely extensible — and still coherent.

---

## How Droplets Work

A Droplet is a self-contained capability that plugs into the Fluid platform and behaves as if it were always part of it. When a Droplet is installed, it inherits the We-Commerce foundation automatically: attribution is respected, payments are trusted, sync remains intact, compliance is enforced. You are not bolting something onto Fluid from the outside. You are building within it.

This is the critical distinction between Droplets and traditional integrations. A typical third-party integration creates a separate data flow — its own system, its own rules, its own failure modes. A Droplet operates inside the Fluid ecosystem, with Fluid as the data owner and source of truth. A Droplet can store its own data, but it always interacts with the Fluid platform and admin to accomplish anything meaningful.

Installation is designed to be simple. For most Droplets, a company provides credentials or configuration details and the Droplet is active. No separate technical build required.

---

## The Droplet Marketplace

The Droplet Marketplace is where companies discover, evaluate, and install Droplets. It is available to all Fluid clients. In principle, any developer or company can access the marketplace by becoming a Fluid user — either as a client company or as a developer building for the ecosystem.

**Currently 20–30 Droplets are live in the marketplace**, including:

- **Avalara** — Tax calculation
- **Sovos** — Tax calculation
- **ShipStation** — Fulfillment
- **ShipHero** — Fulfillment
- **Extended Shipping Calculator** — Advanced shipping logic
- **Exigo, ByDesign, Pillars** — Back office Connect integrations (these are the foundational Droplets that power Fluid Connect)
- Data and reporting Droplets

The marketplace is growing. New Droplets are added regularly by Fluid and by third-party developers.

**Marketplace visibility** is configurable. A Droplet can be made available to all companies in the marketplace, or it can be built for a specific client and kept private. Functionally, private and public Droplets work identically — only the visibility differs.

---

## What Can Be Built with Droplets

**Custom widgets and interfaces** — New UI components, workflows, and experiences that live inside We-Commerce sites and apps.

**Third-party integrations** — CRMs, ERPs, fulfillment providers, analytics platforms, marketing tools — connected to Fluid without breaking attribution or sync.

**New commerce experiences** — Niche flows, vertical-specific tools, or entirely new ways to sell that are native to the We-Commerce cycle.

**Automations and intelligence layers** — Actions triggered by platform events: a purchase, a live stream engagement, a follow-up task, a subscription renewal.

**Partner ecosystems** — Agencies, developers, and technology partners can build reusable Droplets that are deployed across multiple client organizations.

---

## Droplets and Fluid Connect

Connect — Fluid's back office synchronization system — relies entirely on Droplets to function. The integration between a client's commission engine and Fluid is established through a back office Droplet (Exigo, ByDesign, Pillars, etc.). This makes Connect the one Fluid feature that is wholly dependent on the Droplet infrastructure.

From a marketing perspective, this doesn't need to be surfaced in detail. The key message is that connecting to a back office is a one-click process — provide credentials, hit install, and the sync is live.

---

## The Ecosystem Opportunity

The Droplet Marketplace is designed to grow in a way that compounds value over time. As more third parties build Droplets, more capabilities become available to every Fluid client. Companies get access to an expanding library of best-practice tools without waiting for Fluid to build each one.

This mirrors how the most successful platform ecosystems work — the platform provides the foundation, and a community of builders extends it in every direction. The capabilities of the Droplet Marketplace are theoretically limitless.

For Fluid, this also means that as the ecosystem grows, Fluid becomes increasingly central to the direct selling technology landscape — not because it has locked anyone in, but because it has made itself the most useful place to build.

---

## Why Droplets Matter

No platform can anticipate everything its customers will need. The companies that try to build everything themselves eventually become slow, fragmented, and unable to keep up with what the market needs.

Droplets are Fluid's answer to that problem. They allow the platform to grow without losing its integrity — so corporate teams get flexibility without chaos, reps get new capabilities without new tools, and developers get freedom without fragility.

The goal is not openness for its own sake. It is coherent extensibility — the ability to add anything, while the core keeps working.

voice_guide_docs.label: Fluid Connect
voice_guide_docs.content:
# Fluid Connect

## What It Is

Fluid Connect is the data synchronization layer of the We-Commerce platform. It is the connective tissue that keeps a direct selling company's back-office systems — commission engines, product data, customer records, orders — continuously aligned with everything running on Fluid.

Most direct selling companies run their business on a commission engine such as Exigo, ByDesign, or Pillars. These systems are the source of truth for distributor trees, compensation calculations, and compliance. Connect bridges that world with Fluid's commerce experience, ensuring both systems always reflect reality — without manual exports, fragile scripts, or IT dependency.

The official external product name is **Fluid Connect**. Internally it may be referred to as "data sync" or "Sync," but those terms should not be used in marketing or client-facing materials.

---

## How It Works

### One-Click Back Office Connection
Connect uses Droplets — Fluid's extensibility layer — to establish the integration between a client's commission engine and the Fluid platform. A client provides their back office credentials, selects the appropriate Droplet (e.g., the Exigo Droplet, the ByDesign Droplet), and the connection is live. This is the one part of the Fluid platform where Droplets are foundational rather than optional: the back office Droplet is what makes Connect possible.

### Bi-Directional Data Flow
Connect moves data in both directions. Orders and customer activity generated on Fluid flow downstream into the commission engine. Product data, pricing, distributor trees, and compensation logic flow upstream into the Fluid platform. Companies can configure which system is the source of truth for each data type and whether syncs run automatically or are triggered manually.

### Automatic Syncing
Changes made in either system are reflected across the We-Commerce ecosystem without delay. Pricing updates, product availability changes, and new distributor enrollments stay consistent across web, mobile, live shopping, and rep-shared experiences — without requiring a manual export or an IT ticket.

### Complex Data Mapping
Direct selling back office data structures are often highly customized. Connect is built to handle that complexity. It maps a client's existing data structure — however non-standard — to the Fluid platform without requiring a system rebuild. This includes support for Exigo Pillars, ByDesign, InfoTrax, and other common commission engine architectures.

### Real-Time Visibility
Connect surfaces the status of every sync job in a clean dashboard — what ran, what succeeded, what failed, and why. If something breaks, the team sees it immediately and can act on it. This replaces the previous reality for most MLM companies: hidden server scripts, command-line tools, and a single IT person who knows where everything lives.

---

## Key Capabilities

**Automatic and manual sync options** — Companies can schedule syncs, run them automatically on a set frequency, or trigger them manually for specific use cases.

**Two-way sync with conflict resolution** — Clients choose which system wins for each data field in cases where both systems have conflicting values.

**Support for all major commission engines** — Exigo, ByDesign, Pillars, InfoTrax, and others are supported via dedicated Connect Droplets in the marketplace.

**Attribution preserved end-to-end** — FairShare attribution data travels with transactions as they flow into the commission engine, ensuring the right reps are credited downstream without reconciliation.

**Reduced operational drag** — Fewer manual imports. Fewer mismatched records. Fewer "why doesn't this match?" conversations between corporate and the field.

---

## On the Roadmap: Back Office Migration

Connect is building toward a capability that does not yet exist in the market: the ability for a direct selling company to migrate from one commission engine to another — for example, from ByDesign to Exigo — with minimal disruption and no data loss.

Many direct selling companies are effectively locked into their current back office because the cost and risk of migration feels prohibitive. Legacy data structures, customized compensation trees, and years of transaction history make switching feel impossible.

Fluid Connect is designed to remove that lock-in. The approved way to describe this capability in marketing materials is: **"Migrate between back offices without data loss."**

This feature is still in development and should not be presented as currently available. It can be referenced as a future capability or direction.

---

## Why It Matters

Most of the tools direct selling companies use to sync their back office to other systems were built by internal IT teams — shell scripts, scheduled exports, SFTP folders — and are understood by one or two people. When those people leave, the company is exposed.

Connect replaces all of that with one transparent, managed system. It is not trying to be impressive. It is trying to be dependable. And in commerce, dependability is what scales.

When systems agree, people trust them. When people trust the system, they move faster.

---

## Competitive Context

Traditional e-commerce platforms like Shopify have no native concept of a commission engine integration. The complex compensation logic that defines direct selling — downline attribution, CV/QV calculations, rank qualification — is not something Shopify was built to handle. Companies attempting to run a direct selling operation on Shopify are forced to build and maintain custom sync infrastructure on their own.

Connect makes this a solved problem, not an ongoing maintenance burden.

voice_guide_docs.label: Fluid Payments
voice_guide_docs.content:
# Fluid Payments

## What It Is

Fluid Payments is the payment infrastructure layer of the We-Commerce platform. It is built specifically for the realities of direct selling commerce — higher-risk merchant classification, complex attribution requirements, international markets, subscription billing, and enrollment orders — rather than adapted from a generic e-commerce payment stack.

The official product name is **Fluid Payments**. Within this, **Fluid Pay** is a specific saved payment method product (analogous to Shop Pay), distinct from the broader Fluid Payments infrastructure.

Fluid Payments is not just a payment processor. It is a payment orchestration system — managing multiple processors, routing intelligently, recovering failed transactions, and ensuring every legitimate purchase has the best possible chance of going through.

---

## Core Philosophy

In traditional e-commerce, a 2–3% payment failure rate is considered acceptable. Platforms are playing a volume game where individual declined transactions are statistics.

Direct selling is different. A failed transaction isn't just a lost sale. It's a rep's contact — someone they've been nurturing for weeks. It's a new distributor's first order. It's a subscription that lapses and doesn't come back. The compound cost of a declined transaction in direct selling is far greater than in anonymous e-commerce.

Fluid Payments was built around a single principle: **every transaction matters.**

---

## How It Works

### Cascading Payment Retries
When a transaction is declined, Fluid Payments doesn't stop. It automatically retries the transaction in real time across multiple payment processors. Each processor has different card network rules, bank relationships, and approval logic. What fails on one processor may succeed on another — immediately, without the customer needing to do anything.

This is different from decline recovery (see below). Cascading retries are real-time. The customer never knows it happened.

### Decline Recovery
Decline recovery is a separate, more strategic capability that operates on a different timeline from cascading retries. When a transaction cannot be recovered in real time, decline recovery attempts to recover it over a period of days or weeks — using different data elements, responding to specific decline reason codes, and retrying on an intelligent schedule.

Fluid Payments integrates with specialized third-party decline recovery vendors and makes their capabilities available to merchants directly through the platform — no separate technical integration required on the merchant's side.

Cascading retries and decline recovery are two distinct features. Cascading retries are real-time, processor-level. Decline recovery is strategic, over time.

### Subscription Recovery
For subscription transactions specifically, Fluid Payments includes dedicated subscription recovery logic — ensuring that if a recurring charge fails, the system works to recover it rather than simply lapsing the subscription.

### Payment Orchestration (Rules-Based, Moving to AI)
Fluid Payments uses a multi-processor architecture with intelligent routing logic. Currently, routing decisions are rules-based — the system evaluates transaction characteristics and routes to the processor most likely to approve. AI-driven orchestration is coming soon and will further optimize routing dynamically.

### Network Tokens
Fluid Payments supports network tokens — a relatively new and not yet widely adopted payment technology. Network tokens are created by a customer's issuing bank and are unique to the Fluid platform and its merchants. This means subsequent purchases from that customer are more likely to be approved and carry a significantly lower fraud rate.

The tokens are owned by Fluid's merchants — not by Fluid, and not by a third-party processor — which is unusual in the industry and represents a meaningful long-term advantage. This is a strong value proposition for merchants who process high volume or operate in markets with elevated fraud rates.

### One-Click Checkout and Alternative Payment Methods
Fluid Payments includes a full suite of alternative payment methods (APMs) that are pre-integrated and available globally. These remove friction at the moment of purchase — the moment where intent is highest and friction is most costly.

**Currently live APMs include:** Apple Pay, PayPal, Fastlane (PayPal accelerated checkout), AliPay, GCash, iDEAL, Bancontact, Blik, P24, and others. Additional APMs are added regularly.

Fluid Pay is the platform's native saved payment method — similar to Shop Pay — allowing returning customers to check out with a single tap using a previously stored payment method.

### Built for Higher-Risk Commerce
Direct selling merchants are classified as higher-risk by payment processors. This means their baseline decline rates are already worse than a typical e-commerce merchant. Fluid Payments is built with this classification in mind — its processor relationships, routing logic, and fraud tools are calibrated for the direct selling context rather than generic e-commerce.

### Attribution-Aware Payments
Fluid Payments is integrated directly with FairShare. Every successful transaction is automatically tied to the correct rep attribution at the point of payment — no reconciliation, no post-processing, no disputes. The credit assignment happens as part of the payment flow itself.

---

## What's Coming

**AI-Driven Orchestration** — Payment routing will soon be optimized dynamically by AI rather than static rules, further improving approval rates.

**Cash Reconciliation** — Important for merchants running multiple payment processors, cash reconciliation is in development and will provide a unified view of funds across payment sources.

---

## Key Differentiators

- Multiple live processors with real-time cascading retry — not a single-processor solution
- Network tokens: merchant-owned, bank-issued, dramatically lower fraud rates
- Decline recovery via specialized vendors, integrated directly into the platform
- Full APM suite with global coverage, growing monthly
- Fastlane for PayPal accelerated checkout
- Subscription recovery built in
- Attribution embedded in the payment flow — no separate reconciliation
- Built for the higher-risk classification of direct selling, not adapted from generic e-commerce

---

## Competitive Context

Shopify Payments and Stripe are built for anonymous e-commerce transactions — guest checkouts, single-step purchases, standard merchant classifications. They are not built for the layered complexity of direct selling: recurring subscriptions tied to compensation plans, enrollment orders with downline attribution, international transactions that need to route through rep-specific commission logic.

The result for direct selling companies using generic payment infrastructure is predictable: higher decline rates, lost subscriptions, broken attribution, and frustrated reps who learn that sending customers to the website is a gamble.

Fluid Payments exists to make that gamble into a certainty — or as close to one as payment infrastructure can get.

voice_guide_docs.label: FairShare
voice_guide_docs.content:
# FairShare

## What It Is

FairShare is Fluid's attribution system — built specifically for direct selling — that tracks the full customer journey and ensures credit goes to the reps who created the outcome. Not just whoever was last in the chain. Not just whoever owns the domain. But whoever actually showed up.

In direct selling, a purchase is rarely a single moment. It's the result of a shared link, a live demo, a follow-up message, a product recommendation three weeks ago. Traditional e-commerce only sees the last click. FairShare is built to honor the whole journey.

FairShare is not a feature companies turn on. It is the foundational belief the entire Fluid platform is built on: that when people's contributions are recognized, they keep contributing.

---

## How Attribution Works

FairShare tracks meaningful interactions — shared links, live streams, product views, media engagement, and follow-ups — and uses configurable rules to determine who receives credit when a purchase occurs. All attribution happens automatically at checkout, with no manual reconciliation required.

### The Four Attribution Settings

All four settings are live today and fully configurable per company.

**1. Shared Links**
When a rep shares a link and a customer purchases as a result, the company determines which rep receives credit. Options: First Touch (the rep who first introduced the customer), Last Touch (the rep whose link was most recently used), or Most Active (the rep with the highest interaction volume with that customer).

**2. Sponsored Ordering**
When a customer returns to the site and places a new order, FairShare identifies their sponsor from their previous order history and assigns credit accordingly. Companies can configure this behavior in three ways: honor the original sponsor, bypass the sponsor and credit a rep who shared a link to the customer, or replace the sponsor entirely so the sharing rep becomes the new sponsor.

**3. Orphan Orders**
When a customer arrives without any rep interaction or attribution — no shared link, no sponsor — the company decides what happens to that order. Options: leave the customer unattributed (orphaned), assign all orphan orders to one designated rep, or distribute orphan credit among the top-sharing reps in the company.

**4. Enrollment Credit**
When a new rep enrolls, the company decides who receives credit for that enrollment order: the new rep themselves, or their new sponsor.

**5. Manual Setting**
A fifth option allows companies to require customers to manually select a rep at checkout before completing their purchase. This is live but not commonly recommended — FairShare's automatic logic typically makes it unnecessary and removes friction from the customer experience.

---

## Order Journey

The Order Journey is FairShare's attribution audit trail — the official record of every interaction a customer had on their path to purchase.

The Order Journey displays every rep a customer came into contact with, every action taken, and the timestamps of each event. When attribution is disputed — when two reps both believe they deserve credit — the Order Journey is the source of truth. It removes the subjectivity from the conversation entirely.

Companies can view the Order Journey in the admin. It shows exactly what happened, in what order, and who should receive credit based on the company's configured rules.

---

## Media, Playlists, and Content Attribution

Everything shared through the Fluid platform carries FairShare attribution — not just product links.

**Media** — Videos, images, and promotional content can be shared by both reps and corporate. When a customer engages with shared media and proceeds to purchase, FairShare tracks that path and credits the rep who shared it.

**Playlists** — Both reps and corporate can curate playlists: collections of products, media, and content organized around a theme (a promotion, a product category, a seasonal campaign). When a playlist is shared, FairShare attribution is automatically attached. Any purchase that results from engagement with that playlist credits the rep who shared it.

**Any Page on the Website** — Through a global embed in the Builder, FairShare attribution is applied to every page of the company's website. This means a rep can share any page — the homepage, a product quiz, the "Shop All" page, a brand story page — and FairShare will track and credit any purchase that follows. Reps are not limited to sharing product-specific links.

---

## The Replicated Site

Every rep has access to a FairShare-powered replica of the full company website. Every page, every product, every piece of content on the company's site is automatically connected to that rep. When a customer visits through a rep's replicated site link, all activity is attributed to that rep — across sessions, across devices, and even if the customer returns days later through a Google search.

This is distinct from the MySite (see Rep Tools), which is a simpler, rep-curated storefront. The replicated site is the full company website, attributed to the rep.

---

## The SDK

FairShare includes an SDK (Software Development Kit) for enterprise scenarios where a company cannot migrate their entire web presence to Fluid but wants to implement FairShare attribution on their existing site. The SDK allows the FairShare attribution engine, cart, and chat widget to be embedded into a non-Fluid website.

This capability is live but is not a primary marketing focus. It is relevant for large enterprise clients evaluating partial adoption before a full platform transition.

---

## Payments and Attribution, Unified

FairShare is embedded directly into Fluid Payments. Attribution is resolved at the point of checkout — not after the fact, not through a separate reconciliation process. When a transaction completes, the correct rep is credited automatically, and that attribution data flows downstream into the commission engine via Fluid Connect.

---

## Why FairShare Exists

Direct selling only works when people believe their effort matters.

When attribution is unclear, trust erodes. Reps stop sharing corporate content because they've been burned — they sent someone to the website, someone else got credit, and they learned that the system doesn't work for them. So they stop using it.

FairShare exists to fix that permanently. Not by adding a tracking code reps have to remember, or a spreadsheet someone has to maintain, but by making accurate attribution the automatic default for everything.

When reps trust the system, they share. When they share, traffic grows. When traffic grows, conversions follow. The entire We-Commerce flywheel depends on reps believing that their contribution will be recognized — and FairShare is what makes that belief reasonable.

voice_guide_docs.label: Corporate Tools
voice_guide_docs.content:
# Corporate Tools

## What They Are

Corporate Tools are the administrative and management capabilities within the Fluid platform that allow corporate teams — marketing, operations, compliance, IT — to build, govern, and evolve the entire We-Commerce system without engineering bottlenecks.

In direct selling, scaling requires control. But control that slows the organization down defeats its purpose. Corporate Tools are built around a specific tension: giving corporate teams the ability to define standards, manage the system, and maintain brand integrity — without requiring that every change go through a developer or an agency.

The result is an organization that can move at the speed of the business.

---

## The Web Builder

Corporate teams use the Web Builder (also called Builder) to design and manage the public website — the primary digital presence where customers shop, where reps send their contacts, and where the brand lives.

The Builder is a drag-and-drop tool that does not require engineering support for day-to-day updates. Campaign pages, product launches, promotional banners, enrollment flow changes, and full site redesigns can all be executed by a non-technical marketing or operations team member.

Key capabilities:

- **Section library** — Pre-built, high-converting sections that can be dropped into any page
- **Multiple themes** — Maintain multiple site designs simultaneously, with one active at a time; switch the live theme without losing content
- **Version history and rollback** — All changes are versioned; previous states are restorable
- **FairShare integration** — Every page built in the Builder automatically carries FairShare attribution, so any page the corporate team creates can be shared by reps with attribution intact
- **International support** — Multi-language content and regional layout configurations
- **No developer required** — Marketing teams execute changes directly without filing tickets

---

## The App Builder and Mobile App Builder

Corporate teams also control the rep-facing app experience through two builders:

**Mobile App Builder** — Designs the mobile app experience for reps: what features are shown, how the interface is organized, what content is available by country, language, or rank.

**App Builder** — Designs the web portal version of the rep experience (the Rep Portal). The App Builder is the newer tool and is expected to eventually manage both web and mobile rep experiences from a single interface.

Both builders allow:

- Drag-and-drop layout customization
- Country, language, and rank-specific content variations
- Branded visual assets and color schemes
- Instant deployment — no app store approval delay

---

## Guardrails, Not Restrictions

Corporate Tools operate on a guardrails model rather than a restriction model. Corporate defines what is possible — the brand parameters, the approved content, the available features. Reps and field leaders work within those boundaries with meaningful freedom to personalize their experience and their content.

This is a meaningful distinction. Restrictions prevent action. Guardrails channel it. Corporate gets brand integrity without micromanagement. Reps get ownership without chaos.

---

## Admin and Order Management

Corporate teams have full visibility into and control over the commerce layer:

- **Order management** — Full order history, filtering, refund processing, and fulfillment management including split and held fulfillment
- **Subscription management** — Admin can take any action on subscriptions that a customer can take, including modifying billing dates, updating payment methods, pausing, and canceling
- **Enrollment management** — Enrollment forms, country-specific configurations, and pre-seeded international agreements are all managed in the admin
- **Promo code engine** — Create and manage promotional codes with full configurability by discount type, country, customer type, product, and timing
- **Order on Behalf Of** — Corporate and rep admin can place orders on behalf of customers and assign credit to the appropriate rep
- **Inventory** — Product availability management with bulk edit and warehouse support

---

## Sync and Data Management (Connect)

Corporate teams control the Fluid Connect configuration — the settings that determine how data flows between the Fluid platform and the company's commission engine. This includes sync scheduling, conflict resolution rules, and real-time status monitoring of all sync jobs.

---

## International Operations

Fluid's admin is built for global operations from day one. Adding a new country or region to the platform activates:

- Country-specific pricing (at the variant level)
- Market-appropriate payment methods (APMs configured per country)
- Localized enrollment forms and pre-seeded compliance agreements
- Language-specific content within the Builder
- Country-specific promo code and product availability settings

International expansion is a managed configuration process, not a rebuild.

---

## Why It Matters

Corporate teams in direct selling companies are often the bottleneck — not because they are slow, but because the tools they have require developer support for changes that should take minutes. Every product launch that requires an IT ticket. Every campaign that waits for an agency. Every region that needs a custom build.

Fluid's Corporate Tools are designed to remove those bottlenecks — not by eliminating oversight, but by giving the people closest to the business the ability to act on it directly.

When corporate can move quickly and confidently, the whole organization moves with it.

voice_guide_docs.label: App Rep Tools
voice_guide_docs.content:
# App (Rep Tools)

## What It Is

Fluid's App is the rep-facing commerce and business management tool — available on both mobile and desktop — that gives distributors everything they need to share content, follow up with contacts, manage their business, and get paid, without switching between platforms.
